attrs.disease: Cold sores (orolabial) & genital herpes; keratitis; HSV-1 encephalitis (temporal lobe); neonatal disseminated disease
attrs.virulence: Glycoproteins for entry/fusion; immune evasion via cell-to-cell spread; LAT maintains latency
attrs.treatment: Acyclovir / valacyclovir (nucleoside analogues activated by viral thymidine kinase)
attrs.special: CD8 T-cells + IFN-γ maintain latency; reactivation by stress/UV/immunosuppression

attrs.disease: Chickenpox (varicella) primary; shingles (herpes zoster) on reactivation
attrs.virulence: Spreads cell-to-cell; carried by infected T-lymphocytes to skin
attrs.vaccine: Yes — live attenuated (varicella) + recombinant subunit (gE) zoster vaccine
attrs.special: Reactivates as zoster (single dermatome) when immunity declines

attrs.disease: Infectious mononucleosis; Burkitt lymphoma; nasopharyngeal carcinoma; Hodgkin lymphoma; oral hairy leukoplakia (HIV)
attrs.virulence: Stimulates B-cell growth & immortalization; EBNA-1 maintains genome
attrs.special: Mono = overreactive T-cell response; Burkitt = t(8;14) c-myc translocation; Downey cells

attrs.transmission: Transplacental, breast milk, saliva, sexual, transfusion, transplant
attrs.disease: Mostly asymptomatic; mononucleosis-like (heterophile-negative); congenital (deafness, vision, IQ deficits); retinitis/colitis/pneumonia in immunocompromised
attrs.virulence: Inhibits MHC expression and NK cells; immunosuppressive cytokines
attrs.special: "Owl’s eye" intranuclear inclusions; worst congenital outcomes with primary maternal infection

attrs.disease: Kaposi sarcoma (AIDS-associated); B-cell lymphoma
attrs.keyVF: Viral homologs of IL-6, Bcl-2, chemokines
attrs.virulence: Encodes human-like proteins (IL-6 homolog, Bcl-2 analog) driving proliferation
attrs.special: Opportunistic; associated with AIDS

attrs.disease: Pharyngo-conjunctivitis; epidemic keratoconjunctivitis; acute respiratory disease; hemorrhagic cystitis (children); gastroenteritis
attrs.virulence: E1A binds p105RB, E1B binds p53 → drive cell growth; E3 prevents MHC-I presentation
attrs.treatment: No specific therapy; ribavirin/cidofovir in immunocompromised
attrs.special: E1A/E1B transform animal cells (not oncogenic in humans); naked → environmentally stable; used as gene-therapy vector

attrs.disease: Erythema infectiosum (fifth disease, "slapped cheek"); aplastic crisis (in hemolytic anemia); hydrops fetalis
attrs.virulence: Infects erythroid precursors in bone marrow → suppresses erythropoiesis
attrs.treatment: Supportive; IV immunoglobulin / transfusion in severe cases
attrs.special: Needs mitotically active cells; 2-phase disease; greatest fetal risk in 2nd trimester

attrs.transmission: Direct skin/mucosal contact; sexual; birth canal
attrs.disease: Warts & condylomas (6,11); cervical, oropharyngeal, anal, penile cancer (16,18)
attrs.virulence: E6→p53 degradation, E7→Rb inactivation, E5→enhances EGFR; oncogenesis when genome integrates (disrupts E2)
attrs.treatment: No specific antiviral; cidofovir for recurrent lesions
attrs.special: Life cycle tied to keratinocyte differentiation; no viral polymerase; Pap smear / koilocytes

attrs.disease: JC → progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML); BK → nephropathy & hemorrhagic cystitis (transplant)
attrs.virulence: T-Ag inactivates p53 and pRb
attrs.special: Reactivate in immunocompromised; SV40 T-Ag causes tumors in animals but not humans

attrs.disease: Smallpox (eradicated); molluscum contagiosum; monkeypox; orf/cowpox
attrs.keyVF: Carries own DNA-dependent RNA polymerase; immune-evasion genes (~30% of genome)
attrs.virulence: Encodes chemokine/cytokine homologs to evade immunity; spreads cell-to-cell
attrs.special: UNIQUE: the only DNA virus that replicates in the CYTOPLASM (brings its own RNA polymerase)

attrs.disease: AIDS — progressive CD4 depletion → opportunistic infections, dementia, tumors
attrs.keyVF: Reverse transcriptase; gp120 (CD4/CCR5 binding); Nef (downregulates MHC-I)
attrs.virulence: gp120 binds CD4 + CCR5/CXCR4; Nef diverts MHC-I to lysosome; high antigenic variation; integrates into genome
attrs.treatment: HAART: NRTI/NNRTI (RT), integrase inhibitors, protease inhibitors, entry (maraviroc)/fusion (T20) inhibitors
attrs.special: Protease inhibitors block virion MATURATION; PrEP (TDF/FTC); huge mutation rate hinders vaccine

attrs.disease: Adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATL); HTLV-associated myelopathy / tropical spastic paraparesis (HAM/TSP)
attrs.virulence: TAX drives proliferation & DNA damage; HBZ promotes survival; spreads via virological synapse
attrs.special: Most carriers asymptomatic; long latency, onset in adulthood

attrs.disease: Influenza (fever, myalgia, cough); primary viral / secondary bacterial pneumonia; myocarditis
attrs.virulence: HA binds sialic acid (entry); NA releases virions; M2 ion channel for uncoating; cap-snatching
attrs.treatment: NA inhibitors (oseltamivir, zanamivir); baloxavir (cap-snatching); M2 blockers (amantadine, often resistant)
attrs.special: Antigenic SHIFT (reassortment of segments → pandemics) vs DRIFT (point mutations → seasonal); A & B cause recurrent outbreaks

attrs.disease: Measles (maculopapular rash, cough, conjunctivitis, Koplik spots); subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) — late/rare
attrs.virulence: Infects lymphocytes/epithelium; suppresses immunity (kills monocytes, T/B cells); rash is T-cell mediated
attrs.treatment: Vitamin A; supportive; IgG for immunocompromised
attrs.special: Replication begins in the RESPIRATORY tract; SSPE = defective virus persisting in CNS

attrs.disease: Parotitis (parotid swelling); orchitis; aseptic meningitis; pancreatitis
attrs.virulence: Spreads systemically via viremia; cell-to-cell spread
attrs.special: Only one serotype; lifelong immunity; host range limited to humans

attrs.disease: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) in children; common-cold-like illness
attrs.virulence: Local respiratory infection; short-lived immunity → reinfection
attrs.special: Spread remains local (no viremia)

attrs.disease: Bronchiolitis & pneumonia in infants (leading cause of fatal pediatric ARI); cold in adults
attrs.virulence: Localized respiratory infection; bronchiolitis largely immune-mediated; no viremia
attrs.vaccine: Partial — maternal/older-adult vaccines & monoclonal Ab prophylaxis
attrs.special: Main risk group: pediatric; maternal Ab insufficient to protect newborns

attrs.transmission: Bite of infected animal (zoonosis: bats, dogs, foxes)
attrs.disease: Rabies — prodrome → encephalitis with hydrophobia, hyperactivity → coma → death
attrs.keyVF: Retrograde axonal transport to CNS; binds AChR/NCAM
attrs.virulence: Replicates slowly at bite site, travels retrograde along nerves to CNS, then to salivary glands
attrs.treatment: Post-exposure prophylaxis: HRIG near wound + 4 doses inactivated vaccine (effective only before CNS involvement)
attrs.special: Negri bodies in neurons; once symptomatic, almost invariably fatal

attrs.disease: COVID-19 (respiratory; multisystem in severe disease)
attrs.keyVF: Spike protein (binds ACE2); needs proteases to activate spike
attrs.virulence: Spike–ACE2 entry; direct + immune-mediated injury; cytokine release
attrs.treatment: Protease inhibitors (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, molnupiravir); polymerase (remdesivir); monoclonal Ab; corticosteroids/IL-6 inhibitors
attrs.special: SARS-CoV (2003) caused MORE symptomatic infections → easier to contain → did not become pandemic

attrs.disease: Poliomyelitis: mostly asymptomatic; aseptic meningitis; flaccid paralysis (motor neuron infection); post-polio syndrome
attrs.virulence: Translated as polyprotein; replicates in gut then CNS motor neurons
attrs.special: Post-polio syndrome = late muscle dysfunction years after infection

attrs.disease: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease; herpangina; meningitis/encephalitis; myocarditis; pericarditis
attrs.virulence: Do not affect motor neurons; main cause of viral CNS infection in children
attrs.special: Most infections subclinical

attrs.disease: Common cold (upper respiratory tract)
attrs.virulence: Limited to upper airway; symptoms via local inflammation (TNF-α, interleukins)
attrs.special: >100 serotypes (hard to vaccinate); temperature-dependent (does not grow >33 °C) → limited to upper airway; labile to acid pH

attrs.disease: Acute viral hepatitis (self-limiting; NO chronic infection)
attrs.virulence: Replicates in intestine → liver; immune-mediated hepatocyte damage; non-lytic release in exosomes
attrs.special: Highly resistant (acid pH, drying, 4 °C); does not cause chronic infection

attrs.disease: Gastroenteritis in young children (leading cause of severe pediatric diarrhea)
attrs.virulence: NSP4 raises Ca²⁺ → disrupts tight junctions & villi → secretory diarrhea & malabsorption
attrs.special: Segmented genome → reassortment; double/triple capsid; environmentally stable

attrs.disease: Acute gastroenteritis outbreaks (watery diarrhea, vomiting)
attrs.virulence: Extremely contagious; infects small-intestine epithelium (little inflammation)
attrs.special: Short-lived immunity + many strains → recurrent infections

attrs.disease: Dengue fever; dengue hemorrhagic fever (DHF) / dengue shock syndrome (DSS)
attrs.virulence: Replicates in monocytes/macrophages; ADE on second infection with a DIFFERENT serotype worsens disease
attrs.special: Prior infection with a DIFFERENT serotype increases risk of severe disease (ADE)

attrs.disease: Yellow fever — high viremia, hepatotropism (jaundice), hemorrhage, multi-organ failure
attrs.virulence: Strong tropism for hepatocytes → apoptosis; dysregulated cytokine release → shock
attrs.special: Sylvatic (monkey↔mosquito) and urban (human↔Aedes aegypti) cycles

attrs.disease: Usually mild (flu-like, rash, conjunctivitis); congenital microcephaly
attrs.virulence: Broad tropism (CNS, gonads, placenta); surface glycoprotein binds neuronal receptors → fetal neuronal apoptosis
attrs.special: Cross-reacts serologically with dengue; prior Zika may worsen later dengue (and vice versa)

attrs.disease: Mostly flu-like; encephalitis (crosses BBB)
attrs.virulence: Infects dermal DCs/macrophages → lymph nodes → viremia; can cross BBB → neurons
attrs.special: IgM may persist up to a year

attrs.disease: Biphasic: flu-like phase → meningoencephalitis / cranial nerve paralysis
attrs.virulence: Replicates in myeloid cells → viremia → crosses BBB
attrs.special: Biphasic course with asymptomatic interval

attrs.disease: Often chronic hepatitis → cirrhosis → hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)
attrs.virulence: ~70% become chronic; carcinogenesis by persistent inflammation (no viral oncogene)
attrs.treatment: Direct-acting antivirals targeting NS3/4A protease, NS5A, NS5B polymerase (curative combinations)
attrs.special: Not an arbovirus (despite Flaviviridae); enters via LDL receptors; anti-HCV Ab not protective

attrs.disease: Fever, severe polyarthralgia / polyarthritis, myalgia
attrs.virulence: Reaches bloodstream → macrophages → fibroblasts; immune response adds tissue damage
attrs.special: Highly cytolytic in human cells; persistent/non-cytolytic in insect cells

attrs.disease: Rubella (German measles): mild rash/lymphadenopathy; congenital rubella syndrome (severe malformations)
attrs.virulence: Not cytolytic; disrupts growth/mitosis of fetal cells; crosses placenta → fetal malformations
attrs.special: Transplacental transmission → most dangerous to fetus (malformations, premature birth)

attrs.transmission: Direct contact with body fluids (bats = likely reservoir)
attrs.disease: Ebola hemorrhagic fever — diffuse hemorrhage, hypovolemic shock, high mortality
attrs.virulence: Replicates in myeloid/endothelial cells & DCs; triggers out-of-control inflammation; decoy GP neutralizes antibodies
attrs.special: Decoy glycoprotein distracts the immune system (similar to HBV)

attrs.disease: Hepatitis B — 90% resolve; chronic → cirrhosis → hepatocellular carcinoma
attrs.keyVF: HBsAg subviral decoy particles; reverse transcriptase
attrs.virulence: Produces excess HBsAg subviral particles (immune decoy); chronic immune injury (CD8 exhaustion, immune complexes)
attrs.treatment: Reverse-transcriptase inhibitors (tenofovir/entecavir); anti-HBV Ig for newborns of HBsAg+ mothers
attrs.special: A DNA virus that uses REVERSE TRANSCRIPTION (atypical retrovirus, Baltimore group VII) → treatable with RT inhibitors

attrs.disease: Worsens hepatitis B; ~40% of fulminant hepatitis
attrs.virulence: Defective — needs HBV surface antigen (HBsAg) to complete its cycle
attrs.vaccine: Partial — HBV vaccine prevents (no HBV → no HDV)
attrs.special: Superinfection (on chronic HBV) → high chronicity; co-infection → usually self-limited

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses has a segmented RNA genome?
questions.explanation: Influenza (Orthomyxoviridae) has a segmented (8-piece) −ssRNA genome → antigenic shift by reassortment.

questions.stem: Baltimore’s virus classification system is based on:

options.t: The strategy of replication (genome type & mRNA production)

questions.explanation: Baltimore groups viruses by nucleic acid type and how they make mRNA (replication strategy).

questions.stem: Which of these viruses can be treated with a reverse transcriptase inhibitor?
questions.explanation: HIV (retrovirus) and HBV (uses reverse transcription, Baltimore VII) are targeted by RT inhibitors.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a naked virus (without a lipid envelope)?
questions.explanation: Poliovirus (Picornaviridae) is naked; the others are enveloped.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses is transmitted primarily via the transplacental route?
questions.explanation: Rubella crosses the placenta → congenital rubella syndrome.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses is NOT transmitted to the fetus during pregnancy?
questions.explanation: HPV is generally acquired in the birth canal, not transplacentally; Parvovirus, CMV and HIV can cross the placenta.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses can establish latency in B lymphocytes?
questions.explanation: EBV establishes latency in memory B-cells (VZV → neurons, CMV → myeloid cells).

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses has NOT been associated with cancer in humans?
questions.explanation: VZV is not oncogenic; HCV/HBV → hepatocellular carcinoma, HTLV-1 → adult T-cell leukemia.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses most commonly causes gastroenteritis in pediatric age?
questions.explanation: Rotavirus is the leading cause of severe diarrhea in young children.

questions.stem: For which of the following viruses is a prophylactic vaccine available?
questions.explanation: A live oral rotavirus vaccine exists; there is no licensed HIV, CMV or HCV vaccine.

questions.stem: Which viruses cause recurrent influenza outbreaks?
questions.explanation: Influenza A and B drive recurrent seasonal epidemics.

questions.stem: Which statement explains the low ability of avian influenza H5N1 to infect humans?

options.t: The sialic-acid receptor it uses is expressed only in the deeper human airways
Humans develop very effective antibodies against H5N1
The human innate immune system is very effective against H5N1
The receptor is not expressed in humans at all

questions.explanation: H5N1 prefers an α-2,3 sialic acid found mainly in the lower/deeper human airways, limiting efficient infection/transmission.

questions.stem: Which virus is associated with hand-foot-and-mouth disease?
questions.explanation: Coxsackievirus (Enterovirus) causes hand-foot-and-mouth disease.

questions.stem: Which late but rare complication is associated with measles virus infection?
questions.explanation: SSPE is a rare, late CNS complication caused by a defective measles virus persisting in the brain.

questions.stem: What is a target of the new antiviral drugs against HCV?
questions.explanation: Direct-acting antivirals target NS5A (and NS3/4A protease, NS5B polymerase).

questions.stem: Where does replication of the picornavirus genome occur?

options.t: In the cytoplasm of the host cell
In the nucleus of the host cell

questions.explanation: Picornaviruses (+ssRNA) replicate in the cytoplasm.

questions.stem: What types of infections are caused by adenoviruses?

options.t: All of these types of infection

questions.explanation: Adenoviruses cause respiratory, genitourinary (hemorrhagic cystitis) and enteric (gastroenteritis) infections.

questions.stem: Which stage of the HIV replicative cycle is inhibited by protease inhibitors?

options.t: Entry of the virus into the cell
Viral fusion with the cell membrane

questions.explanation: Protease inhibitors block cleavage of the Gag-Pol polyprotein → block virion maturation (non-infectious particles).

questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes the latent form of the varicella-zoster virus?

options.t: It remains latent in the neurons of the sensory ganglia
It remains latent in T lymphocytes
It remains latent in skin epithelial cells
It remains latent in B lymphocytes

questions.explanation: VZV becomes latent in sensory (dorsal root / cranial) ganglion neurons; reactivation = zoster.

questions.stem: Thirty years after a viral infection, a patient is diagnosed with hepatocellular carcinoma. Which virus most likely caused the infection?
questions.explanation: Chronic HCV (and HBV) → cirrhosis → hepatocellular carcinoma over decades; HAV/HEV do not cause chronic infection.

questions.stem: Why did the 2003 SARS-CoV virus not become pandemic like SARS-CoV-2?

options.t: SARS-CoV caused more symptomatic infections than SARS-CoV-2
SARS-CoV was less resistant in the environment
SARS-CoV used a different receptor than SARS-CoV-2
SARS-CoV elicited a weaker immune response

questions.explanation: SARS-CoV caused symptoms before/with transmissibility, so cases were easier to identify and isolate → contained; SARS-CoV-2 has many asymptomatic spreaders.

questions.stem: Which of the following human viruses belongs to the Flaviviridae family?
questions.explanation: Zika is a Flaviviridae (also Dengue, Yellow fever, West Nile, TBEV, HCV).

questions.stem: Which of the following conditions can be associated with an HTLV-1 infection?
questions.explanation: HTLV-1 → adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (and HAM/TSP). Burkitt → EBV; fetal hydrops → Parvovirus B19.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses must incorporate (carry) an RNA polymerase within the viral particle?
questions.explanation: Negative-sense RNA viruses (rabies, influenza) package an RdRp because their genome cannot be translated directly. +ssRNA (polio) is translated directly; HIV carries reverse transcriptase, not an RdRp.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses replicate in the cell nucleus?
questions.explanation: Influenza and herpesviruses replicate in the nucleus. Picornaviruses replicate in the cytoplasm — and poxvirus is the one DNA virus that replicates in the cytoplasm.

questions.stem: For which of the following viruses can genomic reassortment occur?
questions.explanation: Reassortment requires a segmented genome: influenza (−ssRNA, 8 segments) and rotavirus (dsRNA, segmented).

questions.stem: By what mechanism does HPV cause cancer?
questions.explanation: HPV E6 targets p53 and E7 inactivates Rb, driving the cell into S phase → transformation.

questions.stem: Which diseases are associated with the EBV virus?
questions.explanation: EBV → mononucleosis, Burkitt lymphoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, Hodgkin lymphoma. Adult T-cell leukemia is HTLV-1.

questions.stem: Which component of a viral particle makes it most susceptible to the virucidal action of detergents?

options.t: Surface proteins exposed on the particle

questions.explanation: Detergents/solvents dissolve the lipid envelope → enveloped viruses are labile; naked viruses are resistant.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) virus?
questions.explanation: Reoviridae (incl. rotavirus) are dsRNA; HAV is +ssRNA; HPV is dsDNA; HIV is +ssRNA (RT).

questions.stem: What assay measures, in serum, the immunoglobulins capable of inhibiting infection by a virus?
questions.explanation: The neutralization assay detects antibodies that block viral entry (functional/neutralizing antibodies).

questions.stem: What is the cause of systemic symptoms during infection with an influenza virus?

options.t: Direct tissue damage caused by viral replication

questions.explanation: Systemic flu symptoms (fever, malaise, myalgia) are driven by interferon/cytokine release.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses CANNOT cause the formation of syncytia following infection?
questions.explanation: Syncytia formation requires fusion (envelope) glycoproteins; naked adenovirus cannot form syncytia.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses could be inhibited by agents that prevent the lowering of vesicular (endosomal) pH?
questions.explanation: Influenza uses pH-dependent (endosomal) entry; agents blocking endosomal acidification inhibit uncoating.

questions.stem: Which condition can lead to an increased risk of severe disease following infection with the Dengue virus?

options.t: Previous infection with a different Dengue serotype
Primary infection in the absence of prior immunity
Previous infection with the same Dengue serotype

questions.explanation: Antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE): prior infection with a different serotype enhances viral entry → DHF/DSS.

questions.stem: Which statement regarding the HSV-1 and HSV-2 viruses is NOT correct?

options.t: The humoral immune response is essential for maintaining latency
Immunosuppression can lead to viral reactivation
Both viruses can cause genital lesions
An infected individual can spread the virus even if asymptomatic

questions.explanation: Latency is controlled by CELL-MEDIATED immunity (CD8 T-cells + IFN-γ), not antibodies — so that statement is incorrect.

questions.stem: Where does viral replication begin in measles virus infection?
questions.explanation: Measles begins replicating in the respiratory tract, then disseminates via lymphatics and viremia.

questions.stem: Which of the following characteristics can influence the tropism of a virus?

options.t: The presence of a specific receptor on the cell
The Gram stain of the host cell
The catalase activity of the host cell

questions.explanation: Tropism depends on cellular receptors and on proteases needed to activate viral glycoproteins.

questions.stem: Which of the following drugs are effective against HIV?
questions.explanation: HIV therapy uses RT, integrase, protease and entry/fusion inhibitors. Neuraminidase inhibitors are for influenza.

questions.stem: Which of the following tests can be used to detect HIV infection?
questions.explanation: HIV diagnosis: ELISA screen → Western blot confirmation → NAT/PCR for early detection / neonatal cases.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses can cause chronic infections?
questions.explanation: HBV, HCV and HIV establish chronic/persistent infection; HAV is acute and self-limiting.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses is a DNA virus?
questions.explanation: HPV (Papillomaviridae) is a dsDNA virus; the others are RNA viruses.

questions.stem: What is post-polio syndrome?

options.t: A late deterioration of muscle function years after the original infection
A chronic active poliovirus infection of the gut
A bacterial superinfection of paralyzed muscles
An allergic reaction to the polio vaccine

questions.explanation: Post-polio syndrome = progressive muscle weakness decades later, as compensatory neurons undergo age-related decline.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses are associated with tumors in humans?
questions.explanation: Oncogenic human viruses: HPV (cervical), EBV (Burkitt/NPC), HBV & HCV (HCC), HTLV-1 (ATL), HHV-8 (Kaposi). Rhinovirus is not oncogenic.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses can be transmitted from an animal to a human (zoonosis)?
questions.explanation: Rabies is a classic zoonosis (animal bite). Measles, polio and rubella are exclusively human.
